<!-- Generated by the ccode-to-codex mirror sync on 2026-04-14 from input revision 9a2403fb. -->

# Embedded Agent Taxonomy

Classify embedded Claude agent sites before mapping them. This is a
dependency-classification problem first, not just a primitive-mapping problem.

## 1. Two Classes

| Class | Typical signals | What it means | Migration strategy |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Inline ephemeral agent slice | `Agent({...})` or `Task({...})` carries the full prompt inline; `subagent_type` is generic like `general-purpose`; model/mode live next to the prompt | The behavior is defined inside the skill body or reference package. No external agent file is required. | Keep it inline. Preserve prompt contract, sandbox intent, and model/effort semantics. Convert to Codex `spawn_agent(...)` only when delegation is part of the source behavior. |
| Named file-backed agent dependency | `subagent_type`, `role`, or routing-table targets match `.claude/agents/<name>.md` such as `example-agent-b`, `example-agent-j`, `example-agent-c`, `example-agent-a` | The skill depends on a separate runtime artifact, not just an inline prompt slice. | Treat it as a hard dependency edge. Require `.codex/agents/<name>.toml`. If missing, stop and route that dependency to `migrate-agents-to-codex` before calling the skill migration complete. |

## 2. Detection Rules

- Inline slice indicators:
  - Full prompt text or output contract lives in the skill or its references
  - `subagent_type: "general-purpose"` or similarly generic scope labels
  - Model and mode are declared right next to the prompt
- Named dependency indicators:
  - `subagent_type: "<name>"` where `<name>` matches `.claude/agents/<name>.md`
  - Routing tables or heuristics emit named agents such as `example-agent-b`
  - The skill tells the parent to "launch `<agent>`" rather than defining the role inline
- Routing tables are dependency multipliers:
  - If a phase can resolve to several named agents, every reachable named agent must be checked against `.codex/agents/`
  - Do not mark the skill native while one branch still depends on an unmigrated Claude agent
  - Do not substitute a reachable named agent with a different agent just to clear the dependency

## 3. Inline Model / Effort Preservation

Authoritative reference:

- Use `.codex/skills/migrate-agents-to-codex/references/subagent-migration-guide.md`
  for intent-based model mapping when hand-authoring a blocked dependency edge.

Use intent-based mapping for embedded sub-agents:

| Embedded job | Suggested Codex parameters | Why |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Discovery / lookup helper | `model: "gpt-5.4-mini"`, `reasoning_effort: "medium"` | Fast, structured text work |
| Diff summarizer / commit helper | `model: "gpt-5.4-mini"`, `reasoning_effort: "medium"` | Small bounded context |
| Focused implementation helper | `model: "gpt-5.4"`, `reasoning_effort: "medium"` | Default coding worker |
| Adversarial review / example-agent-g | `model: "gpt-5.4"`, `reasoning_effort: "high"` | Risk-focused deeper reasoning |
| High-volume batch worker | `model: "gpt-5.3-codex-spark"`, `reasoning_effort: "high"` | Throughput-oriented micro-tasks |
| Parent-session mirror | Omit model override | Preserve parent runtime choice |

Rules:

- Map by job intent first, not by Claude label alone.
- Merge explicit effort hints after choosing the runtime class.
- Do not silently normalize every embedded agent to the same Codex runtime.

## 4. Named Dependency Gate

For every named agent dependency:

1. Confirm `.claude/agents/<name>.md` exists.
2. Check for `.codex/agents/<name>.toml`.
3. If the Codex agent exists:
   - Keep the dependency explicit in the migrated skill.
   - Reference the Codex agent path in execution guidance or the consumption contract.
   - Spawn the sub-agent against that contract; do not collapse it into a generic worker description.
4. If the Codex agent does not exist:
   - Stop dependency resolution for that edge.
   - Route the missing agent through `migrate-agents-to-codex` at
     `.codex/skills/migrate-agents-to-codex/SKILL.md`.
   - Keep the skill status blocked until the dependency is resolved.
   - Never substitute the named dependency with another agent, a generic worker, or prose-only guidance.

## 5. Output Patterns

### Inline agent slice

Source shape:

- Plan discovery or focused implementation uses a generic agent plus a fully
  inlined prompt and explicit model hint.

Migration shape:

- Preserve the delegated slice inline in the skill body.
- Keep the prompt contract local to the skill or its reference package.
- Map model/effort intent explicitly in the `spawn_agent(...)` example.

Lifecycle preservation:

- If the source uses one bounded delegated pass, preserve it as a one-shot
  helper: `spawn_agent(...)` + `wait_agent(...)`.
- If the source reuses the same helper across multiple rounds, preserve it as a
  persistent helper: `spawn_agent(...)` + `send_input(...)` + `wait_agent(...)`
  + `close_agent(...)`.
- If the source explicitly ends helper lifetime, preserve that with
  `close_agent(...)` instead of leaving cleanup implicit.
- Keep `message` as the default payload shape for inline slices.
- Only escalate to structured `items` when the source really carries typed or
  multimodal context that would be lossy in plain text.
- Only use `fork_context` when inheriting the parent conversational state is
  part of the original behavior rather than a convenience during migration.

### Named file-backed dependency

Source shape:

- Review, remediation, debugging, or routing tables resolve to agent names that
  exist under `.claude/agents/`.

Migration shape:

- Tell the parent workflow to read `.codex/agents/<name>.toml`.
- Keep the agent file as a visible dependency in references or the consumption contract.
- If that file is missing, route to `migrate-agents-to-codex` at
  `.codex/skills/migrate-agents-to-codex/SKILL.md` instead of pretending the
  skill is fully migrated.
- Do not downgrade the dependency to a different named agent or a generic `spawn_agent(...)` slice.
